title: Kozo paper in bookbinding
subtitle: Why Japanese kozo tissue is used for document repair, spine lining, and hinge reinforcement
excerpt: Kozo paper is made from the inner bark fibre of the paper mulberry tree. Its long interlocking fibres produce a sheet that is simultaneously thin, transparent, and extraordinarily strong. These properties make it the first choice in conservation bookbinding for mending torn pages, reinforcing spine linings, and hinging board reattachments.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain), chapter on Japanese papermaking and the washi tradition. Internet Archive scan available. Hunter describes kozo fibre properties in detail. The application in bookbinding conservation is synthesised from consistent contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The inner bark fibre of the paper mulberry tree (Broussonetia papyrifera), used in the Japanese washi tradition to make long-fibre paper of exceptional strength.
Paper made from unusually long cellulose fibres that interlock across the sheet structure, giving the paper its characteristic combination of thinness and tensile strength.
A bookbinding repair that reinforces or replaces the flexible zone between a text block's spine and its cover board.

content.text: Kozo paper has been made in Japan for over a thousand years. The fibre comes from the inner bark of Broussonetia papyrifera, the paper mulberry tree. The individual fibres are 5 to 10 times longer than cotton linter fibres, and they interlock during sheet-forming in a way that creates a paper with remarkable tensile strength for its weight.
Why it outperforms cotton tissue in repairs

does not tear cleanly along a line the way short-fibre paper does. When kozo tissue bridges a tear in a document, the tissue fibres and the document fibres interlock at the adhesive joint. This spreads any future stress across a wide zone instead of concentrating it at the repair edge. A cotton-tissue repair has shorter fibres and produces a harder edge at the repair zone. That hard edge can become a new crack line.
Grades of kozo and when to use them
6 to 10 gsm tissue: the thinnest available, used for transparent document mending. Virtually invisible on a printed page when applied with rice paste adhesive.
12 to 18 gsm: the standard repair tissue for torn leaves in hand-bound books. Strong enough to hold a torn folio flat; thin enough not to distort the page when viewed.
25 to 40 gsm: used as a hinge strip for
and as a secondary spine lining under book mull in conservation rebinds.
Applying kozo tissue with rice paste

tissue to size rather than cutting it: a torn edge interleaves with the document fibre at the joint; a cut edge does not. Apply Japanese rice paste (nori) rather than PVA: rice paste is reversible with water, which is essential in conservation work. Smooth the patch flat with a bone folder through a light-release paper and allow to dry under a flat weight.
For the bookbinder without conservation goals

Outside conservation work, kozo tissue is still useful to the craft bookbinder. A strip of 25 gsm kozo applied around the first and last signatures of a text block before sewing acts as a hinge reinforcement that distributes the tension of the sewing thread. A spine lining of 12 gsm kozo applied before the standard mull lining produces a very flexible spine that opens well for heavily used journals.
